pageCaption.value: In 2012, Dawa Yangzum Sherpa scaled the highest summit on Earth at 21 years of age. Today, with the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, she runs an annual high-altitude climbing course to provide Nepali women the skills to conquer life’s mountains.

text.value: In 2012, Dawa Yangzum Sherpa scaled the highest summit on Earth at 21 years of age. Today, with the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, she runs an annual high-altitude climbing course to provide Nepali women the skills to conquer life’s mountains.

pageCaption.value: In 2012, Dawa Yangzum Sherpa scaled the highest summit on Earth at 21 years of age. Today, with the support of the Rolex Perpetual Planet Initiative, she runs an annual high-altitude climbing course to provide Nepali women the skills to conquer life’s mountains.

metadescription.value: Supported by Rolex's Perpetual Planet Initiative, Dawa Yangzum is inspiring future female mountaineers through her climbing course. More on rolex.org.

text.value: Nestled among the Himalayas, at 4,200 metres above sea level, the Rolwaling Valley where Dawa Yangzum Sherpa grew up has been home to several notable male climbers. The rugged and remote conditions in high altitudes may give villagers an aptitude for mountaineering but, as a woman, no one expected her to follow this path. At 13 years of age, Dawa Yangzum defied expectations by making her way to Kathmandu where she took the first steps towards her dream of summiting Everest and forging a career on the mountains.

After completing several initial courses and spending time as a long-distance trail runner, Dawa Yangzum found her way to the Khumbu Climbing Center (KCC) where she undertook training in ice and rock climbing. Founded in 2003 by adventurer and alpinist Conrad Anker together with his wife, artist and climber Jennifer Lowe-Anker, the centre is where Nepali climbers are taught technical skills, rescue and first aid to improve the mountain safety of indigenous and local communities. While attending the KCC, Dawa Yangzum caught the attention of Anker who selected her for an expedition to Mount Everest. Two years later, at the age of 21, she stood atop the tallest peak on the planet.

In doing so, Dawa Yangzum had fulfilled her childhood dream. However, as is the case for all adventurers, she knew there were always more mountains to climb. For her, that meant gaining the International Federation of Mountain Guides Associations (IFMGA) certification, a rigorous process to acquire the skills and knowledge needed to lead expeditions in the planet’s most formidable mountain environments. Even for the most elite athletes and experienced professionals, this can take years to complete. In 2017, Dawa Yangzum passed the final exams and became the first Asian woman to earn the prestigious qualification, and one of just over 100 women worldwide. Since then, she has conquered eight of the “eight-thousanders”, the 14 mountains on Earth that are more than 8,000 metres above sea level, and also spends time in the United States where she is a guide. She was part of the first all-female team from Nepal to ascend K2, known as the “Savage Mountain”, the deadliest peak in the world.

On her journey from a Himalayan village with no electricity or running water, to the top of the highest peaks, Dawa Yangzum encountered many challenges and setbacks but credits those who helped her along the way. She says: “I met many mentors who helped me out of that situation. I will always remember them.”

Just as she benefited from the assistance and support of others, she decided to do the same for the next wave of young girls like her, initiating a positive cycle that could ripple down generations of Nepali women. “I too want to be a mentor who can help people, especially women, take their first steps towards their dreams.”
I too want to be a mentor who can help people, especially women, take their first steps towards their dreams.

Keen to give back to her community, in 2019, Dawa Yangzum founded an annual two-week ice and rock-climbing course for Nepali women and girls that takes place at the KCC. The skills she teaches are invaluable to her students, not just for those who go on to work as trekking guides or continue to climb for fun, but for all, as a way to help navigate life’s challenges. In keeping with its long-standing tradition of supporting intergenerational mentoring, Rolex supports Dawa Yangzum’s training course through its Perpetual Planet Initiative.

This year, eight students were taught about clothing and gear, safety, logistics and technique, as well as first aid, map reading and geology. It is an extensive, wide-ranging course and what they learn is broader still – patience, resilience, focus and a joy in being outdoors. Dawa Yangzum has also found her students gain a lot of self-belief and a sense of independence. The girls who turn up are often shy and quiet, but after the fortnight they leave as more communicative and self-assured people. “Day by day, you can clearly see the change.”

An important part of this is the confidence the students build by learning what their bodies are capable of. Disproving the preconception that girls aren’t strong enough, Dawa Yangzum shows them how a mix of technique, dedication and persistence is the key to success. She finds the pupils are always quick learners and gain experience they can take forward into their careers. “They need to love what they’re doing to become really good at their job.”
Day by day, you can clearly see the change.

Dawa Yangzum has also benefited from running the course. By becoming a role model, she has proved there are no limits to her own ambitions. After focusing on her passion for a decade, she can now see her hard work and perseverance through the eyes of a beginner again. One of Dawa Yangzum’s star students, Rashila – also a successful long-distance runner – has already made significant progress since starting to climb just a few years ago and is now an advanced student at KCC.

For Dawa Yangzum, it is fulfilling to watch someone just like her at the outset of an incredible journey in the role of mentor, something from which she benefited hugely and that was so vital to her own progress and eventual success.

For the girls and women who take Dawa Yangzum’s course each year, she empowers them to pursue their dreams – whatever they may be – just as she did. One day, when they have scaled mountains of their own, they might also find themselves returning to teach the next generation.

pageTitle.value: Alison Criscitiello and Mount Logan - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: For almost a century, Rolex has championed pioneering explorers who are advancing the boundaries of what is possible. Through its Perpetual Planet Initiative, Rolex supports those with a new mission: to understand how we can preserve our natural world.

WaPageTitle.value: CANADA EXPEDITION FINDS RECORD ICE CORE

metadescription.value: National Geographic Explorer Alison Criscitiello is uncovering the long climate record of Mount Logan through ice core analysis. More on rolex.org.

pageCaption.value: Local people from one of the world’s wildest and most isolated places, the mountainous Himalaya region of Humla in Nepal, are being enlisted as frontline conservators to rescue dwindling wild animal populations – from snow leopards to wild yaks.

pageCaption.value: In May 2019, as part of its Perpetual Planet partnership with Rolex, National Geographic succeeded in leading an expedition to install the world’s highest weather station on Mt Everest.
